runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model context.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. In the third decile, individuals may face higher sensitivity due to the phase-out of the EITC, but structural constraints on hours usually keep the intensive-margin response relatively low, typically estimated between 0.1 and 0.2.

  "interpretation": "Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. microsimulation model context.",

    "p25": 0.08,
    "p50": 0.12,
    "p75": 0.20,
    "p95": 0.35
  },
  "citations": [
    "Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012)",
    "Chetty (2012)",
    "CBO (2012) Labor Supply Elasticities"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners typically exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. In the third decile, individuals may face higher sensitivity due to the phase-out of the EITC, but structural constraints on hours usually keep the intensive-margin response relatively low, typically estimated between 0.1 and 0.2."
}

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. For individuals in the 3rd decile (typically low-to-moderate income), there is slightly more responsiveness than at the very top of the distribution due to potential intensive margin adjustments and the influence of the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) phase-out ranges, though point estimates remain modest in the literature.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Primary-earner substitution elasticity for those in the 3rd earnings decile in a U.S. microsimulation model context.",
  "point_estimate": 0.15,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.05,
    "p25": 0.1,
    "p50": 0.15,
    "p75": 0.25,
    "p95": 0.45
  },
  "citations": [
    "Chetty et al. (2011)",
    "Saez (2010)",
    "Congressional Budget Office (CBO) Labor Supply Elasticity Reports"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners generally exhibit lower labor supply elasticities than secondary earners. For individuals in the 3rd decile (typically low-to-moderate income), there is slightly more responsiveness than at the very top of the distribution due to potential intensive margin adjustments and the influence of the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) phase-out ranges, though point estimates remain modest in the literature."
}

runs.interpretation: The compensated substitution-response elasticity for primary earners in the 3rd earnings decile within a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation context.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. Decile 3 represents lower-income earners who may face significant marginal tax rates due to benefit phase-outs (e.g., EITC), but their intensive-margin labor supply response remains constrained by institutional factors and fixed work costs.

Decile 3 represents lower-income earners who may face significant marginal tax rates due to benefit phase-outs (e.g., EITC), but their intensive-margin labor supply response remains constrained by institutional factors and fixed work costs."

runs.interpretation: The compensated substitution elasticity of labor supply for primary earners in the third earnings decile in the United States, as parameterized in a microsimulation context.
citations: Congressional Budget Office (CBO) labor supply assumptions
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. In the third decile, individuals often face complex incentives due to the phase-out of the EITC, but their intensive margin response remains relatively inelastic compared to higher earners or secondary earners, though slightly higher than the top deciles due to lower hours-fixity.

In the third decile, individuals often face complex incentives due to the phase-out of the EITC, but their intensive margin response remains relatively inelastic compared to higher earners or secondary earners, though slightly higher than the top deciles due to lower hours-fixity."
}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation model, representing the response of labor supply to changes in the marginal net-of-tax wage rate.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. For the third decile, there is significant interaction with the phase-out and phase-in regions of the EITC, but the intensive-margin substitution remains relatively inelastic compared to higher earners who have more control over the timing and form of compensation, or lower-income individuals on the extensive margin.

  "interpretation": "Primary-earner substitution elasticity for earnings decile 3 in a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation model, representing the response of labor supply to changes in the marginal net-of-tax wage rate.",

  ],

}
